Proximal renal tubular acidosis does indeed affect bicarbonate but also results in hyperchloremia because it leads to excessive urinary losses of hydrogen ions with minimal changes in serum chloride - thus resulting more often in high AG metabolic acidosis unless other factors like compensatory mechanisms kick in.

However, I believe option A "proximal RTA" could still technically fit within scenarios where there isn't much change in overall anion gap if associated conditions such as volume depletion from vomiting/diarrhea exacerbate bicarb loss disproportionately compared to Cl-, potentially yielding near-normal AG despite primary proximal RTA pathology.
